So you said you won in district court. In Boise. In Boise. And then after that, in appeals court, it was struck down. So then what was the course of action after that? Uh, they just went ahead with ten reintroductions. Okay. Very, very shortly they went. They almost immediately went up to Canada and picked up those wolves. This talks a little bit about Pre-introduction. I like I said, I forgot I had this. Uh, what is that document just for folks listening. Well, you can cut out what you want. Okay. But, um. I'd. I'd like you to read it. I'd like to get it back. Oh, yeah? Well, certainly. It talks mostly about the wolves after they were introduced. Okay. What happened to him? What happened to him? Which is another piece of evidence that there were wolves here before the reintroduction was at least half of the wolves that they reintroduced mated with wild animals. That's in the report. It's not in this one, but it's in the. Committee reports at the time I don't remember what what that would be called, but no, it was documented that they found wild mates. So your primary interaction, uh, with, you know, congressional members or representatives here at the state or in the federal government. Do you remember any names specifically or any committees, uh, that you worked with or testified in front of or reviewed there. Specifically for wolves? Not wolves. We testified in some, uh, wilderness hearings and, uh, some, uh, roadless area review hearings and, uh. Uh, rare to, uh. Uh, hearings. I think they would call those. And I'm just curious about your, uh, your, you know, where are you going for to stay up to date, I guess, on information around, uh, the actions of, you know, us fish or. Uh, I was on their mailing list. Yeah, they would send me when they were going to. They'd send me scoping documents and, uh, draft EIS or EES and, uh, finished or EES. Environmental environmental assessment is a lower, uh, decision making category. And then environmental impact statement is a it's a bigger thing. So you had been recognized was that an interested party or. Uh, yeah. Yeah. Okay. So after reintroduction happened, when they bring these wolves down from Canada, uh, what was your feeling or, uh, observation about how the whole thing unfolded? Because, you see, the Nez Perce got involved pretty soon after that, right? Yeah, they did a nice job. They did a really nice job. Did you ever work with the. No I didn't. Okay. And the reason? Um. My science advisor, Jim Paek, I call him that. He was a friend, but we talked wolves, and, uh, he told me that. And he was for the totally for the reintroduction effort. He was behind TNG, and he said his his his take on it was, uh. You know, if we can just get him here at all, they're going to survive after that. Right now, they're not. They're getting shot every time, you know, every time the newspaper article comes out. Well, that pack dies. And he said, if we can just get some kind of, you know, population going here, it will survive. He said, because the reality is, is that and I'm not going to be precise because I don't think imprecise, especially numbers. But he said something like. It's well documented that. A wolf pack can sustain something in the neighborhood of 70% mortality and still survive. Wow. They breed like wolves. And actually, when you when you when you when you kill the alpha male or female, they actually breed more interesting. They if you if you destroy the integrity of the pack, they basically all breed prior. If the pack's in integral, they will only the alpha male and alpha female is the only one who will breed predominantly. Interesting. So, uh, what was your interaction, I guess, as this whole process unfolded over the course of the 90s and into the 2000. Were you interested? Were you had you moved on to other topics? After I moved on with the as soon as the reintroduction occurred, I actually did another year or two because they hadn't moved up into North Idaho yet. So I continued to document wolves until there were there were wolves from the reintroduction down on the salmon River that moved into this area. So this is all under the Gray Wolf Committee. And how did that organization operate differently or did it from friends in Clearwater? Was it just you or. It was pretty much the same. Same. They used to call us the many headed serpent. Explain that. Well, because I was friends with the Clearwater, I was the Gray Wolf Committee. I was the Clearwater Forest Watch, and I don't even know what else. I can't even some other things too, I'm sure I oh, I was the friends of the Ponderay. Just, you know, they called us the many headed serpent. Yes. So with the the documentation of wolves moving from salmon up in this way, uh, what were you doing with that information? Was that just kind of for for. Well, I was just watching to see when they would arrive in the Clearwater. At which point I felt like Jim Peak was probably right, that they were here and they could sustain 70% mortality and they would still be here. So what's your feeling, uh, about delisting that came later in 2011, I believe. Well, they were never listed south of the 95. They were never listed. Highway 95. Yeah, They have. They had a boundary of where they were going to re-enter their boundary that was separated by a highway. So were they always classified under Tengeh or that? Never. That never changed. Never changed. Okay. So the whole time it was just this designated area which was essentially the Frank church. No, it was everything south of highway 95. South of. Even in Montana. Oh, really? Well, why? What's the. Don't ask me. There's a highway they can't cross. Oh, you were mentioning earlier that there was evidence that they had. Maybe there was carcass wolf carcasses found on that highway. Ah. So they claim that none of them made it across. Well, I haven't been keeping an eye on the on the clock, but is there anything that I missed or glossed over that you want to, uh, go go through. Doesn't have to be wolf related. Oh, you know, the stories go on forever. Is there one around your time doing wolf work that sticks out? Oh, well, there's well, I have to go back and talk about the violence for the wolf. There was some violence involved with the wolf. I guess the original name of the Grey Wolf Committee was the. Cove Mallard. Grey wolf committee? I don't know if you know what Cove Mallard is. For the for the name. That's another group. That was one of my many headed serpents. That was me at one time. That was me in, uh, Randall. Restless and, uh, bikini. Uh, it was a nonviolent civil disobedience campaign to stop a timber sale of 120mi² near Dixie, Idaho, which we won. It's supposed to be one of the most. Famous. One of the most productive, non-violent, civil disobedience timber sale campaigns in the in the West. I think one of them, anyway. And, uh, they had a once again, my numbers are all a little bit wrong, but it's easy to remember because this way it's not precise. I think there were 120mi² that they were going to clearcut small clear cuts. So they didn't, you know, they met the definition of the law and rode extensively. Rode. And my recollection is, is that when we ground them to a standstill in about 96 or 97, they pulled out. They said they, they, they had some kind of statement to us or something like from where the sun now stands. And as long as the grass is green and, you know, that's what they said, but it was like, okay, you win, we're not going to come back here and do this. And they're trying they're trying that area, right. They're going to log that area right now. But anyway, the Gray Wolf Committee started out as an offshoot of the Cove Mallard coalition. And uh. Our first, uh. Winter kill survey was, uh, on the salmon River near Dixie, and we went into the, uh. Mallard Creek drainage, Rut Creek, and Mallard Creek drainage down to the river in the middle of the winter. And we had to park. We had we had to come to a. We were driven to, uh, Dixie, Idaho, and, uh. There were, uh, because of the Cove Mallard coalition. There were a bunch of very angry people there, and they came out and blocked the cars. And luckily I had set it up ahead of time so that we would just get dropped off because I didn't trust we could keep a car there anyway. But I had it set up that we would just get dropped off there, and then we would snowshoe into, uh, Wreck Creek from there and, uh, down to the salmon River over Churchill Mountain. And, uh. We were met there with a group of people that were. Loud and abusive and one drunk guy. What was his name? Emmitt Smith. Some drunk asshole. Emmitt Smith came out with a 357. Pulled it out of his holster and he was waving it around. He was drunk and drunk, as you could imagine. And they took the gun away from him. So there was there was, uh, violence with the wolf thing, too. But it was it was more I think it was more associated with the Cove Mallard coalition, which was a timber sale. Yeah. Action. Yeah. That's that's I mean, was it par for the course at that time to, you know, similar to now, but uh, you, you know, uh, Oregon Public Radio has the whole thing about the timber wars. And so it's kind of a recounting of all this stuff. Um, Boise Public Radio just recently put out a whole piece, uh, about wolf reintroduction called howl. I saw that, I heard that, yeah. I don't usually listen to podcasts, but I did. I heard about that, and it was pretty nice. Yeah. What'd you think? I haven't listened. I didn't listen to all of them. I listened to the last one, I think. It seemed. Seemed fair. Yeah. Um, you being up here and participating in in the same kind of, uh, advocacy and then, you know, scientific investigation. Did you see any difference from kind of the mainline narrative, uh, with your experience or. I don't understand the question. Well, I guess, um, I imagine you had had a different vantage because I think I was telling you at the beginning of this, I feel like the main story has been out there for a while. It's been told over and over in some ways, and it doesn't seem like there's, uh, you know, maybe your voice or some other folks, uh, who weren't maybe necessarily like Ed Banks comes up a lot. Some of these big names come up a lot. Uh, but there are other people involved. And, um, I was curious, you know, if you're kind of looking at that history or the mainline narrative strikes you as leaving out any important. Oh, yeah. I don't know how to maybe put that to words, but, uh, know that the history is the government's version of the history, and. It seems a little tainted to me. What would you like people to know about, um, doing the work that you did and how that how that played a role or maybe better. What was your what was your goal in doing it? My goal was for Wolves to come back with full protection as they deserve. My goal was for there to be all the predators here that were here when I was a boy. All the animals, all the animals, all the, all the, all the plants, all the animals, you know, it was it was a wildlife Paradise in 1950. And it's not anymore. So when did you, uh, uh, step down from the friends of the Clearwater? After I got mugged, it was hard to. I couldn't be around people for a long time. After that. You were mugged in. The ten drunk loggers that punched a hole in my head with a brass knuckles, and they had guns and knives. Yeah. It was hard to hard to hard to talk around, you know, hard to I was I remember particularly it was hard to be around a group of about ten people. It was just I could not do that. And that's I'd been in large groups before that. So what's your what are you what's your involvement now with the organization? Oh, I'm Treasurer, friends of the Clearwater. I'm working on, um. Going to go to, uh, Upper Luxor and look for grizzly bear females with cubs. Hopefully. This week. This Tuesday. I'd like to. I'd like to I'd like to be the one that documents the first cub in Idaho. Nice. And and that would be. And that would. I hope nobody's listening to this. That, uh. Could do something about this that would. It's going to happen soon. Just like the wolves. It's only a matter of time. And, uh, that would, uh, reject the option for them. Totally. Yeah. And I'm doing a, uh, trying to figure out how to. Establish endangered. Species protection for the Chinook salmon in Bear Valley, which, like I said, there were 200,000 of them when I was there in the 70s, and there's 200 of them there now. I went we went up there and looked, and I was so shocked I decided I would start working on. They're a threatened species at this point in time on the ESA, and with only 300 in Bear Valley Creek, they definitely qualify as endangered and they haven't been put on that protective level. Yeah, and that's purely a political decision. No doubt. Steve, I think we'll wrap it. I think it's about noon. But, uh, if you're amenable, I've been pestering everybody who I've spoken with. If you have, if you would like to do it again, uh, I'd be more than happy to set up a time. And maybe we can do it, even though it feels very nice today. Yeah, maybe we'll do it indoors next time. Well, I I'm amenable, but I think I've said about everything I can say about it. I don't know, I think you have quite, quite the, uh, untapped wealth of knowledge that if we just, uh, explored your time, you know, doing environmental advocacy work and friends of the water, I think there's there's a history there in and of itself that needs to be explored. Well, thank you for taking the time today. Yeah. Appreciate it. Steven. Thanks. Bye.

lines.line: # Introduction and Early Life

> The interview begins with an introduction of Steve Paulson, founder of Friends of the Clearwater, and a brief overview of his family history in Idaho. Steve shares stories about his grandfather's arrival in the Boise Valley around 1910, his upbringing in southern Idaho, and the family's move to California for economic reasons. He describes his strong attachment to Idaho, his repeated attempts to return, and his eventual move back after high school.

metadata.title: Introduction and Early Life
metadata.synopsis: The interview begins with an introduction of Steve Paulson, founder of Friends of the Clearwater, and a brief overview of his family history in Idaho. Steve shares stories about his grandfather's arrival in the Boise Valley around 1910, his upbringing in southern Idaho, and the family's move to California for economic reasons. He describes his strong attachment to Idaho, his repeated attempts to return, and his eventual move back after high school.

lines.line: # Forest Service and Marine Corps Experience

> Steve recounts his early adult years in Idaho, spending time in the Sawtooths, hunting, fishing, and eventually being recruited to work for the Forest Service. He describes his initial work on the brush crew, his decision to join the Marine Corps influenced by family tradition, and his experiences in military electronics school. After his service, he briefly returns to California before heading back to Idaho.

metadata.title: Forest Service and Marine Corps Experience
metadata.synopsis: Steve recounts his early adult years in Idaho, spending time in the Sawtooths, hunting, fishing, and eventually being recruited to work for the Forest Service. He describes his initial work on the brush crew, his decision to join the Marine Corps influenced by family tradition, and his experiences in military electronics school. After his service, he briefly returns to California before heading back to Idaho.

lines.line: # Return to Idaho and Early Environmental Observations

> After leaving the Marine Corps, Steve returns to Idaho and resumes work with the Forest Service in Bear Valley. He describes the abundance of wildlife, including salmon and sandhill cranes, and the pristine nature of the area. Steve's observations of the natural environment during this period lay the groundwork for his later environmental concerns.

metadata.title: Return to Idaho and Early Environmental Observations
metadata.synopsis: After leaving the Marine Corps, Steve returns to Idaho and resumes work with the Forest Service in Bear Valley. He describes the abundance of wildlife, including salmon and sandhill cranes, and the pristine nature of the area. Steve's observations of the natural environment during this period lay the groundwork for his later environmental concerns.

lines.line: # Smokejumping and Life in Rural Idaho

> Steve explains the role of a smokejumper, a wildland firefighter trained to parachute into remote fires, and details his ten-year career in the field. He discusses the qualifications needed, the influence of military service on hiring, and his time living in rural Idaho, including raising his son at a lookout station. The chapter highlights the remote and wild nature of the region and Steve's deep connection to it.

metadata.title: Smokejumping and Life in Rural Idaho
metadata.synopsis: Steve explains the role of a smokejumper, a wildland firefighter trained to parachute into remote fires, and details his ten-year career in the field. He discusses the qualifications needed, the influence of military service on hiring, and his time living in rural Idaho, including raising his son at a lookout station. The chapter highlights the remote and wild nature of the region and Steve's deep connection to it.

lines.line: # Environmental Awakening and Early Activism

> Steve reflects on the environmental changes he witnessed in Idaho, particularly the effects of logging and industrialization on wild lands. He describes the loss of pristine forests and wildlife habitats, recounting childhood memories of abundant trout and wild landscapes. These experiences motivate his shift toward environmental advocacy and concern for the preservation of roadless areas.

metadata.title: Environmental Awakening and Early Activism
metadata.synopsis: Steve reflects on the environmental changes he witnessed in Idaho, particularly the effects of logging and industrialization on wild lands. He describes the loss of pristine forests and wildlife habitats, recounting childhood memories of abundant trout and wild landscapes. These experiences motivate his shift toward environmental advocacy and concern for the preservation of roadless areas.

lines.line: # Founding Friends of the Clearwater and Earth First Connections

> Steve discusses his involvement with Earth First in the mid-1980s and the formation of Friends of the Clearwater. He describes attending the Wild Rockies Earth First Winter Rendezvous in Montana, meeting other activists, and the emergence of a regional network focused on forest protection. The chapter highlights the collaborative spirit and organizational strategies that shaped early environmental activism in Idaho and Montana.

metadata.title: Founding Friends of the Clearwater and Earth First Connections
metadata.synopsis: Steve discusses his involvement with Earth First in the mid-1980s and the formation of Friends of the Clearwater. He describes attending the Wild Rockies Earth First Winter Rendezvous in Montana, meeting other activists, and the emergence of a regional network focused on forest protection. The chapter highlights the collaborative spirit and organizational strategies that shaped early environmental activism in Idaho and Montana.

lines.line: # Timber Sale Appeals and Activist Community

> Steve explains how activists were trained to file timber sale appeals, leveraging legal arguments to challenge logging projects. He describes the growth of the activist community, the use of pseudonyms, and the development of Friends of the Clearwater as a group. The chapter illustrates the grassroots tactics and camaraderie among environmental advocates during this period.

metadata.title: Timber Sale Appeals and Activist Community
metadata.synopsis: Steve explains how activists were trained to file timber sale appeals, leveraging legal arguments to challenge logging projects. He describes the growth of the activist community, the use of pseudonyms, and the development of Friends of the Clearwater as a group. The chapter illustrates the grassroots tactics and camaraderie among environmental advocates during this period.

lines.line: # Quaker Beliefs and Political Context

> Steve shares his adoption of Quaker beliefs, emphasizing simplicity, and distinguishes his views from those of political figures like Richard Nixon. He discusses the legislative history of environmental protection, including the roles of Frank Church and other key figures, and reflects on the influence of environmental organizations in shaping policy. The chapter situates Steve's activism within broader political and philosophical contexts.

metadata.title: Quaker Beliefs and Political Context
metadata.synopsis: Steve shares his adoption of Quaker beliefs, emphasizing simplicity, and distinguishes his views from those of political figures like Richard Nixon. He discusses the legislative history of environmental protection, including the roles of Frank Church and other key figures, and reflects on the influence of environmental organizations in shaping policy. The chapter situates Steve's activism within broader political and philosophical contexts.

lines.line: # Interactions with Politicians and Wolf Reintroduction Policy

> Steve recounts his interactions with politicians such as Jim McClure, describing debates over wolf reintroduction and the practical considerations behind the 10(j) rule. He explains McClure's rationale for supporting controlled reintroduction to maintain local oversight and the political dynamics surrounding the issue. The chapter provides insight into the intersection of environmental advocacy and legislative strategy.

metadata.title: Interactions with Politicians and Wolf Reintroduction Policy
metadata.synopsis: Steve recounts his interactions with politicians such as Jim McClure, describing debates over wolf reintroduction and the practical considerations behind the 10(j) rule. He explains McClure's rationale for supporting controlled reintroduction to maintain local oversight and the political dynamics surrounding the issue. The chapter provides insight into the intersection of environmental advocacy and legislative strategy.

lines.line: # Legal Action and Wolf Documentation

> Steve describes the response to the federal government's environmental impact statement and the decision to challenge the 10(j) rule in court. He details the formation of the Gray Wolf Committee, efforts to train volunteers in wolf biology and winter kill surveys, and the strategy to document existing wolf populations prior to official reintroduction. The chapter highlights the blend of scientific research and legal advocacy.

metadata.title: Legal Action and Wolf Documentation
metadata.synopsis: Steve describes the response to the federal government's environmental impact statement and the decision to challenge the 10(j) rule in court. He details the formation of the Gray Wolf Committee, efforts to train volunteers in wolf biology and winter kill surveys, and the strategy to document existing wolf populations prior to official reintroduction. The chapter highlights the blend of scientific research and legal advocacy.

lines.line: # Evidence of Wolves Prior to Reintroduction

> Steve discusses the substantial evidence of wolves in Idaho before official reintroduction, including field observations, scat analysis, and photographic documentation. He describes collaboration with university researchers and the training of local students in wolf biology. The chapter emphasizes the disconnect between political narratives and on-the-ground realities regarding wolf presence.

metadata.title: Evidence of Wolves Prior to Reintroduction
metadata.synopsis: Steve discusses the substantial evidence of wolves in Idaho before official reintroduction, including field observations, scat analysis, and photographic documentation. He describes collaboration with university researchers and the training of local students in wolf biology. The chapter emphasizes the disconnect between political narratives and on-the-ground realities regarding wolf presence.

lines.line: # Lawsuit Outcomes and Political Climate

> Steve explains the outcome of the legal challenge, winning in district court but losing on appeal, which allowed the government to proceed with 10(j) reintroduction. He describes the hostile political climate, including threats and violence faced by activists, and contrasts attitudes among loggers, cattlemen, and urban residents. The chapter underscores the risks and tensions inherent in environmental advocacy during this era.

metadata.title: Lawsuit Outcomes and Political Climate
metadata.synopsis: Steve explains the outcome of the legal challenge, winning in district court but losing on appeal, which allowed the government to proceed with 10(j) reintroduction. He describes the hostile political climate, including threats and violence faced by activists, and contrasts attitudes among loggers, cattlemen, and urban residents. The chapter underscores the risks and tensions inherent in environmental advocacy during this era.

lines.line: # Wolf Reintroduction and Nez Perce Involvement

> Steve reflects on the process of wolf reintroduction, praising the work of the Nez Perce and discussing biological aspects of wolf population resilience. He shares insights from scientific advisors about wolf pack dynamics and the ability of wolves to survive high mortality rates. The chapter explores the practical outcomes of reintroduction and the adaptation of local wildlife management.

metadata.title: Wolf Reintroduction and Nez Perce Involvement
metadata.synopsis: Steve reflects on the process of wolf reintroduction, praising the work of the Nez Perce and discussing biological aspects of wolf population resilience. He shares insights from scientific advisors about wolf pack dynamics and the ability of wolves to survive high mortality rates. The chapter explores the practical outcomes of reintroduction and the adaptation of local wildlife management.

lines.line: # Later Years, Delisting, and Ongoing Advocacy

> Steve discusses the delisting of wolves, the boundaries of protection under the 10(j) rule, and the continued monitoring of wolf populations. He describes his ongoing advocacy work, including efforts to document grizzly bears and protect Chinook salmon, and his role as treasurer of Friends of the Clearwater. The chapter highlights the evolution of his environmental work and commitment to species protection.

metadata.title: Later Years, Delisting, and Ongoing Advocacy
metadata.synopsis: Steve discusses the delisting of wolves, the boundaries of protection under the 10(j) rule, and the continued monitoring of wolf populations. He describes his ongoing advocacy work, including efforts to document grizzly bears and protect Chinook salmon, and his role as treasurer of Friends of the Clearwater. The chapter highlights the evolution of his environmental work and commitment to species protection.

lines.line: # Reflections, Violence, and Legacy

> Steve shares additional stories of violence associated with activism, including confrontations during the Cove Mallard campaign and threats related to timber sales. He reflects on the legacy of his work, the challenges faced, and his hopes for the restoration of wildlife and ecosystems in Idaho. The chapter concludes with his thoughts on the importance of advocacy, the need for full protection of predators, and his ongoing involvement in environmental efforts.

metadata.title: Reflections, Violence, and Legacy
metadata.synopsis: Steve shares additional stories of violence associated with activism, including confrontations during the Cove Mallard campaign and threats related to timber sales. He reflects on the legacy of his work, the challenges faced, and his hopes for the restoration of wildlife and ecosystems in Idaho. The chapter concludes with his thoughts on the importance of advocacy, the need for full protection of predators, and his ongoing involvement in environmental efforts.

story.title: Steve Paulson - Wolf Reintroduction September 29th, 2025
story.description: The interview centers on Steve Paulson's experience as an environmental activist in Idaho, highlighting his formative years, time in the Marine Corps, and involvement with the U.S. Forest Service. Paulson discusses founding the Friends of the Clearwater and his efforts in environmental preservation, focusing on wolf reintroduction in Idaho. He addresses challenges like political opposition and violence faced during activism. The conversation also touches on his legal battles related to wildlife conservation, especially documenting wolf presence prior to their official reintroduction. Paulson's ongoing conservation efforts and reflections on past advocacy punctuate the dialogue.
